title: Eastern Band of Cherokee Historic Lands Reacquisition Act
short_title: Eastern Band of Cherokee Historic Lands Reacquisition Act
plain_summary: This law gives special Cherokee historical lands in Tennessee to the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians to take care of. The lands include important Cherokee sites like museums and memorials that honor Cherokee history and culture. The Tennessee Valley Authority can still control the river and sometimes flood parts of these lands when needed. The Cherokee tribe cannot build casinos or use the land for gambling. The tribe will use these lands mainly to remember and teach people about Cherokee history.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Indian Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Eastern Band of Cherokee Historic Lands Reacquisition Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Land Taken Into Trust for the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians
sections.text: The United States government will hold certain federal lands in trust for the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians. These lands are managed by the Tennessee Valley Authority and located in Monroe County, Tennessee. The lands must be at or above eight hundred twenty feet above sea level on the shores of Tellico Reservoir. Any existing rights that third parties have for roads, utilities, or other purposes will remain in place. The lands include three main areas. First is the Sequoyah museum property, which covers about forty six acres and includes areas shown on a map dated April thirtieth, two thousand fifteen. Second is the support property, which covers about eleven point nine acres as shown on another map from the same date. Third is the Chota memorial property and Tanasi memorial property, which covers about eighteen point two acres and includes all land within a circle that has a radius of eighty six feet from the center of the Chota Memorial, regardless of the elevation of that land. All buildings, memorials, and other improvements on these lands will belong to the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians. Within one year after any land deal is completed, the Tennessee Valley Authority must work with the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians and the Secretary of the Interior to create updated maps. These new maps must show the exact trust lands and any corrections needed. The maps must be sent to the House Committee on Natural Resources and the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs. The elevation measurements in this law are based on a specific measurement system established in nineteen thirty six. All lands taken into trust under this section must follow the conditions described in section five.

sections.title: Section 3  Permanent Easements Taken Into Trust for the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians
sections.text: The United States government will also hold permanent easements in trust for the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians. These easements are for lands below eight hundred twenty feet above sea level in Monroe County, Tennessee, on the shores of Tellico Reservoir. The easements include two areas. First is the Chota peninsula, which covers about eight point five acres as shown on a map dated April thirtieth, two thousand fifteen. Second is the Chota-Tanasi trail, which covers about eleven point four acres as shown on the same map. Within one year after any land deal involving these easements, the Tennessee Valley Authority must work with the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians and the Secretary of the Interior to create updated maps. These new maps must show the exact easement lands and any needed corrections. The maps must be sent to the House Committee on Natural Resources and the Senate Committee on Indian Affairs. The lands covered by these easements must follow the use rights and conditions described in section five.

sections.title: Section 4  Trust Administration and Purposes
sections.text: Except for special rules in section five, all lands covered by this law will be managed under the same laws and rules that apply to other lands held in trust for Indian tribes. Most of the lands covered by this law will be used mainly to honor and explain the history and culture of Indians and for recreational activities. This does not include the support property described in section two. The main uses include running the Sequoyah birthplace memorial and museum, maintaining memorials to Chota and Tanasi as former Cherokee capitals, managing memorials and burial places for remains of the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians and other Cherokee tribes including those moved by the Tennessee Valley Authority under the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act, and explaining the Trail of Tears National Historic Trail. The support property described in section two will be used mainly to support the other trust lands and the tribe's programs related to those lands. This includes classrooms and conference rooms, cultural interpretation and education programs, temporary housing for guests participating in programs or managing the properties, and headquarters offices and support space for the trust properties and programs. The easement lands described in section three will be used for specific purposes. The Chota peninsula easement will be used mainly for a recreational trail from near the parking lot to the Chota Memorial area and beyond to the southern part of the peninsula, including interpretive signs, benches, and other compatible improvements. The Chota-Tanasi trail easement will be used mainly for a recreational trail between the Chota and Tanasi Memorials, including interpretive signs, benches, and other compatible improvements.

sections.title: Section 5  Use Rights, Conditions
sections.text: The Tennessee Valley Authority can temporarily and sometimes flood the lands covered by this law that are below eight hundred twenty four feet above sea level. This includes flooding the road access to those lands that are also below eight hundred twenty four feet above sea level. The Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians can build, own, operate, and maintain certain facilities. They can build water use facilities and structures that are not meant for living in on land between eight hundred fifteen feet and eight hundred twenty feet above sea level next to the Tellico Reservoir, but only with written permission from the Tennessee Valley Authority and following the terms of that approval. They can also build water use facilities between eight hundred fifteen feet above sea level and the waters of Tellico Reservoir and in those waters, but only with written permission from the Tennessee Valley Authority and following the terms of that approval. However, they cannot build other non-living structures on those lands. The Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians can use the trust lands and Tellico Reservoir to get to and from the land, the reservoir waters, and all structures and facilities on or over the land or waters. The Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians cannot use their rights in a way that interferes with the Tennessee Valley Authority's legal program for river control and development. Nothing in this law affects the Tennessee Valley Authority's right to lower the water level of Tellico Reservoir, change the water level as needed to manage the reservoir, or permanently flood lands next to the trust lands that are below eight hundred fifteen feet above sea level. The trust lands will be subject to reasonable entry by Tennessee Valley Authority personnel and agents working in their official roles as needed to carry out the Tennessee Valley Authority's legal program for river control and development. As long as Tennessee Valley Authority operations on the trust lands do not unreasonably interfere with the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians maintaining an appropriate setting for Cherokee history or culture memorials and their operations, the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians must allow the Tennessee Valley Authority to enter the lands to clear, ditch, dredge, and drain the lands, apply larvicides and chemicals, or do bank protection work and build structures needed for public health, flood control, and navigation. All future development of the trust lands must include payment to the Tennessee Valley Authority for loss of hydropower capacity as provided in the Tennessee Valley Authority Flood Control Storage Loss Guideline, unless the Tennessee Valley Authority agrees otherwise. The United States government will not be responsible for any loss or damage from temporarily flooding trust lands, permanently flooding nearby lands as allowed in this section, wave action in Tellico Reservoir, or water level changes for managing Tellico Reservoir. The Tennessee Valley Authority must keep sole and exclusive federal responsibility and liability to pay for and carry out any environmental cleanup required under federal or state law for any land to be taken into trust under this law, as well as assessments to identify any potential dangerous substances on the lands. Before taking land into trust, the Tennessee Valley Authority must assess and notify the Secretary of the Interior and the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians whether any dangerous substances were stored on the lands and if those substances were stored for one year or more, were known to have been released, or were known to have been disposed of on the lands. If the assessment shows that dangerous substances were stored, released, or disposed of on the lands, the Tennessee Valley Authority must include in its notice the type and amount of such dangerous substances, when the storage, release, or disposal happened, and a description of any cleanup actions taken.

sections.title: Section 6  Lands Subject to the Act
sections.text: For the purposes of this law, the term lands subject to this Act means lands and interests in lands including easements that are taken into trust for the benefit of the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians according to this law.

sections.title: Section 7  Gaming Prohibition
sections.text: No class two or class three gaming, as defined in the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act, can be conducted on lands subject to this law. Class two and class three gaming refers to certain types of casino gambling allowed under federal law.
